import { Meta, Markdown, Source } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'; import Feedback from '../../../docs/common/feedback.md'; import * as SortCodeStories from './sort-code.stories'; import { CustomBadge } from 'storybook-addon-tag-badges/manager-helpers' # Sort code Due for brand modernisation

Provides a directive to apply formatting and specific validators to the input component for entering a sort code.

## Usage Import the sort code and the [text input component](./?path=/docs/components-forms-text-input-guide--docs) into your application: ```js imports: [ ..., LgSortCodeDirective, LgInputComponent, LgHintComponent ], ``` and in your HTML: Both the `lgInput` and `lgSortCode` directives have to be present on the input element. The exact `lg-hint` should be copied as well to ensure the correct context is provided to the user. **We recommend using Angular's reactive forms.** ### Validators The sort code directive automatically adds the `required` and `pattern` validators by default. The `pattern` validator checks that the sort code value has the following patterns: `000000`, `00 00 00` or `00-00-00`. See the [form validation guide](./?path=/docs/components-forms-form-validation-guide--docs) for additional information. ### Format of the control value On blur of the input we auto-format the value of the control to `00-00-00`. *** {Feedback}

import { Meta, Markdown, Source } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'; import Feedback from '../../docs/common/feedback.md?raw'; import * as HeroStories from './hero.stories'; import { CustomBadge } from 'storybook-addon-tag-badges/manager-helpers' # Hero Due for brand modernisation

A flexible pattern that is often used to display the most prominent information about a page. The module consists of lots of smaller presentation components that help to map out the common hero use cases.

## Usage Import the hero components in your application: ```js @NgModule({ ... imports: [ ..., LgHeroComponent, etc. ], }); ``` and in your HTML: ### Other hero types and templates You can extend the basic Hero component and create specific Hero layouts and implementations. #### Hero with breadcrumbs Using the `LgHeroCardHeaderComponent` together with `LgBreadcrumbComponent`, some breadcrumbs can be easily added to the hero. Note that you should use the `light` variant of the [breadcrumb component](./?path=/docs/components-breadcrumb-guide--docs), as it's rendering against a dark blue background. ```html
Home Product Details
``` #### Product details This component can used to display product data, for example on a product details page. Note the extensive use of presentation components to achieve the desired layout. ```html
Pension annuity Payroll Reference Number P23456

Your payments have been suspended, please contact us to learn more.

 Last payment (after tax and deductions) £230.20 Payment due 15 Jan 2020 Payment frequency Monthly Tax code 2T Received on 12 Mar 2019 * This is not a guaranteed amount and could be subject to change.
``` ## Additional development details ### LgHeroComponent This is the branded bar which runs across the top of the page. It also controls the functionality to create the 'overlap' between the page content. #### Inputs | Name | Description | Type | Default | Required | |-----------|--------------------------------------------------------------------|:--------:|:-------:|:--------:| | `overlap` | The amount that the page content overlaps the hero component (rem) | `number` | `2` | No | ### LgHeroContentComponent This is the main section of the hero bar, it stretches the full width and is agnostic of grid system. You will need to configure the grid in the contents of this component to match your page layout. #### Configure Grid ```html
...
``` ### LgHeroHeaderComponent This is the top section of the hero bar, it stretches the full width and is agnostic of grid system. It is generally used to house the breadcrumb component if there is one. Similar to `LgHeroContentComponent`, you will need to configure the grid in the contents of this component to match your page layout. ### LgHeroCardComponent A container component for displaying content within the hero area. The hero and hero card components are agnostic of the grid layout of the page. You will need to wrap the hero card component with the specific grid that is required. ### LgHeroCardHeaderComponent This is the primary layout section of the hero component, it is used to contain the title, subtitle and principle value. ### LgHeroCardTitleComponent This is where the main hero title should be provided. It should be located inside the hero header. #### Inputs | Name | Description | Type | Default | Required | |----------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|:--------:|:-------:|:--------:| | `headingLevel` | The level of the hero card heading: `1`, `2`, `3`, `4`, `5`, `6` | `number` | n/a | Yes | ### LgHeroCardSubtitleComponent If the hero has a subtitle it should be located within this component. If a hero has a subtitle it is expected to also implicitly have a title. It should be located inside the hero header. ### LgHeroCardNotificationComponent Displays a notification associated with a product, for example if the product's payments are in 'suspended' state. ### LgHeroCardPrincipleDataPointComponent Sometimes the hero card will be displaying a principle data point. In that case this layout component should be used. It should be located inside the hero header which will controls it's layout ### LgHeroCardPrincipleDataPointValueComponent This is where the value for the data point should be projected. It should be located inside the hero principle data point ### LgHeroCardPrincipleDataPointLabelComponent This is where the label for the data point should be projected. A data point should always have a value, but it may not have a label. It should be located inside the hero principle data point #### Inputs | Name | Description | Type | Default | Required | |----------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------|:--------:|:-------:|:--------:| | `headingLevel` | The level of the heading in the label: `1`, `2`, `3`, `4`, `5`, `6` | `number` | n/a | Yes | ### LgHeroCardContentComponent This is the secondary layout section of the hero component, it is used to contain secondary information. ### LgHeroCardDataPointListComponent When the hero card is being used to display secondary data points, those data points should be wrapped in a list. This element provides the list semantics, it should be contained inside the hero content. ### LgHeroCardDataPointComponent Sometimes the hero card will be displaying one or more secondary data points. In that case this layout component should be used. It should be located inside the hero content which will controls it's layout. One or more data points can be supported. ### LgHeroCardDataPointValueComponent This is where the value for the data point should be projected. It should be located inside the hero data point ### LgHeroCardDataPointLabelComponent This is where the label for the data point should be projected. A data point should always have a value, but it may not have a label. It should be located inside the hero data point #### Inputs | Name | Description | Type | Default | Required | |----------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------|:--------:|:-------:|:--------:| | `headingLevel` | The level of the heading in the label: `1`, `2`, `3`, `4`, `5`, `6` | `number` | n/a | Yes | ### LgHeroCardFooterComponent This where any extra text related to the hero card can be displayed, such as a small print. *** {Feedback}

The side nav allows the user to define a series of navigation items that display content in a predefined area.

## Usage Import the side nav components in your application: ```js @NgModule({ ... imports: [ ..., LgSideNavComponent, etc. ], }); ``` and in your HTML: ```html Overview Personal details Your name, date of birth, marital status and National Insurance Number. Logout ``` ## Additional development details ### LgSideNavBarComponent Here is where the navigation menu items are projected. On mobile devices, the menu is below the content area, instead of to the left. ### LgSideNavBarLinkDirective This `lgSideNavBarLink` directive applies the appropriate styling to the links. It also emits an event when the link is activated. The links, once clicked on, will display their routes' content in the side nav content component. #### Inputs | Name | Description | Type | Default | Required | |------------|---------------------------------------------------------|:---------:|:-------:|:--------:| | `isActive` | specifies whether or not the link is in an active state | `boolean` | `false` | No | ### LgSideNavBarItemComponent This is where the `LgSideNavBarItemHeadingComponent` and `LgSideNavBarItemContentComponent` of the link are projected and positioned. ### LgSideNavBarItemHeadingComponent This is the main label of the link. ### LgSideNavBarItemContentComponent This is used to add a description for the link. ### LgSideNavBarFooterComponent The footer is below the navigation items and can hold different kinds of content (e.g. a button). ### LgSideNavContentComponent This is the predefined area where content is shown. It should always contain a router outlet to be able to display the different nav item routes. *** {Feedback}

helpers-directives-colour-guide--docs.name: Docs
helpers-directives-colour-guide--docs.title: Helpers/Directives/Colour/Guide
helpers-directives-colour-guide--docs.content:
import { Meta, Markdown } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'; import Feedback from '../../docs/common/feedback.md?raw'; # Colour The `lgColour` directive allows you to apply one of four colour modes to any Canopy component. When combined with a theme, it sets design tokens that control the appearance of interactive elements, backgrounds, borders, and text within the component. ## What are Colour Modes? Colour modes define the overall colour palette for a component by setting CSS custom properties (design tokens). Each mode is associated with a brand colour and provides a complete set of design tokens that control the visual appearance of components, including: * **Interactive elements** - Buttons, links, and other interactive components adapt their colours based on the mode * **Backgrounds and surfaces** - The component's background and surface colours are defined by the mode * **Text colours** - Body text, headings, and interactive text adjust to maintain proper contrast * **Borders and accents** - Border colours and accent elements reflect the chosen colour mode ### Available Colour Modes * **`blue`** - The primary brand colour (default). Used for standard components and general purpose content * **`green`** - Typically used for success, positive messaging, or environmentally-focused content * **`red`** - Typically used for errors, critical information, or urgent messaging * **`yellow`** - Typically used for warnings, cautions, or content requiring attention When you apply a colour mode, the directive adds a class like `lg-mode-blue` to the element, which activates a comprehensive set of design tokens specific to that colour. These tokens ensure that all nested components automatically adapt to the colour mode while maintaining accessibility and visual consistency. ## Understanding Themes Themes modify how the colour mode is expressed visually. They control the intensity and contrast of the colours: * **`neutral`** (default) - Light background with coloured accents * **`neutral-inverse`** - Similar to neutral but with inverted contrast * **`subtle`** - Very light, tinted backgrounds with minimal contrast * **`bold`** - Strong, high-contrast appearance with solid colour backgrounds The directive applies both a mode class (e.g., `lg-mode-blue`) and a theme class (e.g., `lg-theme-neutral`) to the element. Together, these classes activate the appropriate design tokens. ## Usage Import the colour directive in your application: ```js import { LgColourDirective } from '@legal-and-general/canopy'; ``` ### Basic Example Apply a colour mode without specifying a theme (uses `neutral` by default): ```html This card uses the blue colour mode with the default neutral theme. ``` ### Using Colour Modes with Themes Combine a colour mode with a specific theme for different visual effects: ```html

This card has a strong green background with high contrast.

This card has a light yellow tint, ideal for gentle warnings.

This card uses red to indicate an error or critical information.

 ``` ## Directive Inputs | Name | Description | Type | Default | Required | |------------|----------------------------|:------:|:-------:|:--------:| | `lgColour` | The colour mode to apply | `string` | `blue` | No | | `lgColourTheme` | The theme variation to apply | `string` | `neutral` | No | ### Available Values **Colour modes:** * `blue` * `green` * `red` * `yellow` **Themes:** * `neutral` * `neutral-inverse` * `subtle` * `bold` ## How It Works When you use the `lgColour` directive, it: 1. Applies a class in the format `lg-mode-{colour}` (e.g., `lg-mode-blue`) 2. Applies a class in the format `lg-theme-{theme}` (e.g., `lg-theme-neutral`) 3. These classes activate CSS custom properties (design tokens) that components use for their styling 4. Child components inherit and respond to these design tokens automatically *** {Feedback}

import { Meta, Markdown } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'; import Feedback from '../../docs/common/feedback.md?raw'; # Grid The grid directives are used to apply grid classes in order to create multi-column layouts. ## Usage Import the grid directives in your application: ```js imports: [ ..., LgGridContainerDirective, LgGridRowDirective, LgGridColDirective ], ``` There are three directives included in this module which can all be added to Canopy or native HTML components. A simple one-column responsive grid which expands to two columns for larger screen sizes could be created with the following markup: ```html
Column 1
Column 2
``` ### Inputs | Name | Description | Type | Default | Required | |-----------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|:-----------------:|:-------:|:--------:| | `lgContainer` | Adds the grid container class to the element | `null` | `null` | No | | `lgRow` | Adds the row class to the element | `null` | `null` | No | | `lgCol` | Specifies the number of columns to fill in a 12 column layout | `number` (`1-12`) | `null` | Yes | | `lgColMd` | Specifies the number of columns to fill in a 12 column layout on a medium sized screen | `number` (`1-12`) | `null` | Yes | | `lgColLg` | Specifies the number of columns to fill in a 12 column layout on a large sized screen | `number` (`1-12`) | `null` | Yes | | `lgColOffset` | Specifies the number of columns to offset in a 12 column layout | `number` (`0-11`) | `null` | Yes | | `lgColMdOffset` | Specifies the number of columns to offset fill in a 12 column layout on a medium sized screen | `number` (`0-11`) | `null` | Yes | | `lgColLgOffset` | Specifies the number of columns to offset fill in a 12 column layout on a large sized screen | `number` (`0-11`) | `null` | Yes | *** {Feedback}

For example, you may need to hide a component on wider viewports, such as desktop. It uses global CSS responsive utility classes to add the relevant classes. It has a sibling directive: `LgShowAt`. ## Usage Import the hide-at directive in your application: ```js @NgModule({ ... imports: [ ..., LgHideAtDirective ], }); ``` and in your HTML: ### Inputs The current available breakpoints are `sm`, `md`, `lg`, `xl`, and `xxl`. | Name | Description | Type | Default | Required | |------------|------------------------------------|:--------:|:-------:|:--------:| | `lgHideAt` | The name of the breakpoint applied | `string` | `null` | No | *** ## Related * [Show at directive](./?path=/docs/helpers-directives-show-at-guide--docs) {Feedback}

import { Meta, Markdown } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'; import Feedback from '../../../docs/common/feedback.md'; import { getDefaultSpacingVariables } from '../../../docs/foundation/spacing-utils'; # Margin This directive allows for a custom margin to be added without the need to write additional CSS. It's useful for overriding the default margin on Canopy components, as well as general use within your app. Using this directive ensures that your margin adheres to the predefined spacing variables and breakpoints in Canopy. The spacing variables are also available as CSS custom properties (CSS variables) which can be viewed in **_canopy/projects/canopy/src/styles/spacing.scss_**. ## Usage Import the margin directive in your application: ```js imports: [ ..., LgMarginDirective ] ``` In your HTML, apply the directive to the relevant component. If the default `lgMargin` attribute has no value, the default margin will remain. You can also override individual margin such as `margin-top` by applying the relevant selector/input (.e.g `LgMarginTop` - see the **inputs table** below). Pass in **Spacing variant** (see below). ```html ``` ## Spacing ### Spacing variants This is set of predefined variants as defined by the `SpacingVariant` type. These are: 
  
    {(() => {
      const spacingVars = getDefaultSpacingVariables();
      const variants = Object.keys(spacingVars)
        .filter((key) => key.startsWith('--space-'))
        .map((key) => key.replace('--space-', ''))
        .sort((a, b) => {
          // Handle 'none' specially
          if (a === 'none') return -1;
          if (b === 'none') return 1;
          // Sort numbers numerically
          return parseInt(a) - parseInt(b);
        })
        .map((variant) => `'${variant}'`)
        .join(', ');

      return `'none', ${variants}`;
    })()}
### Variant examples Apply `8` margin to the bottom ```html ``` Apply `4` margin to the left and right ```html ``` Apply `7` margin all round, but `9` margin to the bottom ```html ``` Apply `6` margin to the top and bottom ```html ``` ### None at breakpoint When using responsive spacing variables, you may need to set margin to none (zero) at a specific breakpoint. Use the `NoneAt` inputs for this: Apply `4` margin on all sides, but set to none at `sm` breakpoint ```html ``` Apply `5` margin to the right, but set to none at the `md` breakpoint ```html ``` Apply `6` margin on top and bottom, but set to none at the `lg` breakpoint ```html ``` #### Multiple breakpoints with arrays You can also pass an **array of breakpoints** to apply none at multiple specific breakpoints: Apply `7` margin on all sides, but set to none at both `sm` and `lg` breakpoints ```html ``` Apply `5` margin to the top, but set to none at `md`, `xl`, and `xxl` breakpoints ```html ``` Apply `6` margin horizontally, but set to none at `sm` and `xxl` breakpoints ```html ``` **Note:** Duplicate breakpoints in the array are automatically filtered out. #### How NoneAt works with responsive spacing The `NoneAt` inputs create **breakpoint-specific overrides** that only apply within a specific breakpoint range. This is particularly useful when working with responsive spacing CSS variables (like `--space-8`) that change values at different breakpoints. **Example scenario:** ```html ``` This will: - Apply `margin: var(--space-8)` which is responsive across all breakpoints - Override to `margin: 0` **only at the `md` breakpoint** (48rem - 63.94rem) - At `lg`, `xl`, and `xxl` breakpoints, `var(--space-8)` takes effect again with the appropriate values for those breakpoints **Important:** The `NoneAt` classes use **range-based media queries** to ensure they only apply at the specified breakpoint, not at larger breakpoints. This allows the responsive spacing variables to work correctly across your design. #### Migration from SpacingResponsive If you were previously using the `SpacingResponsive` object syntax with `'none'`: **Before:** ```html ``` **After:** ```html ``` For scenarios with multiple "none" breakpoints: **Before:** ```html ``` **After:** ```html ``` The new approach is more compatible with responsive CSS variables and provides better specificity control. ## Inputs The current available spacing variants are:

        })

        .join(', ');

import { Meta, Markdown } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks"; import Feedback from '../../docs/common/feedback.md?raw'; import { CustomBadge } from 'storybook-addon-tag-badges/manager-helpers' # Orientation Due for brand modernisation The orientation directive arranges the direct children of the host element in a vertical or horizontal layout, in a responsive way. It accepts an object, where the **key** is a mobile-first breakpoint from the standard list of available breakpoints: `sm`, `md`, `lg`, `xl`, `xxl`. The **value** is from the set of available Orientation options. The current available options for Orientation are `vertical`, `horizontal`, `reverse-vertical` and `reverse-horizontal`. ## Usage Import the orientation directive in your application: ```js @NgModule({ ... imports: [ ..., LgOrientationDirective ], }); ``` In your HTML, apply the directive to the relevant component. If the default `lgOrientation` attribute has no value, the default orientation will remain. Pass in a **responsive orientation object** (see below). ```html ``` ## Responsive orientation ### Responsive orientation object Use a responsive orientation object which will apply different layout orientations at the specified breakpoints, as defined by the `OrientationResponsive` type. Example: ```js { md: "horizontal", lg: "vertical" } ``` Each **key** is a mobile-first breakpoint from the standard list of available breakpoints: `sm`, `md`, `lg`, `xl`, `xxl`. The **value** is from the set of available Orientation options. The current available options for Orientation are `vertical`, `horizontal`, `reverse-vertical` and `reverse-horizontal`. In the example above, the orientation at the `md` breakpoint will be `horizontal`, and at the `lg` will be `vertical`. ### Orientation options Have this example in mind: ```html

Content

 ``` **vertical** - arranges the direct children of the host element in a vertical layout. The children maintain the same order and 'lg-card-hero-img' is displayed first at the top, followed by 'lg-card-content'. **horizontal** - arranges the direct children of the host element in a horizontal layout. The children maintain the same order and 'lg-card-hero-img' is displayed first on the left, followed by 'lg-card-content'. **reverse-vertical** - arranges the direct children of the host element in a **reverse** vertical layout. The children do not maintain the same order and 'lg-card-content' is displayed first at the top, followed by 'lg-card-hero-img'. **reverse-horizontal** - arranges the direct children of the host element in a **reverse** horizontal layout. The children do not maintain the same order and 'lg-card-content' is displayed first on the left, followed by 'lg-card-hero-img'. ### Examples At the `sm` breakpoint apply `vertical` orientation, then at the `md` breakpoint apply `horizontal` orientation. ```html ``` At the `sm` breakpoint apply `vertical` orientation, at the `md` breakpoint apply `reverse-horizontal` orientation, then at the `lg` breakpoint apply `vertical` orientation. ```html ``` ## Inputs The current available orientation variants are `vertical`, `horizontal`, `reverse-vertical` and `reverse-horizontal`. The current available breakpoints are `sm`, `md`, `lg`, `xl`, `xxl` | Name | Description | Type | Default | Required | |-----------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------|:-----------------------:|:-------:|:--------:| | `lgOrientation` | The orientation variant applied: `{ sm: 'vertical' }` | `OrientationResponsive` | `null` | No | *** ## Related * [Promotions pattern](./?path=/docs/patterns-promotions-examples--promotions-general-three-card) * [Margin directive](./?path=/docs/helpers-directives-margin-guide--docs) * [Row gap directive](./?path=/docs/helpers-directives-row-gap-guide--docs) {Feedback}

import { Meta, Markdown } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'; import Feedback from '../../docs/common/feedback.md?raw'; import { CustomBadge } from 'storybook-addon-tag-badges/manager-helpers' # Screen reader alert message Due for brand modernisation This directive allows for temporary visually hidden alert messages to be read out by screen readers. For example when something has finished loading. ## Usage Import the screen reader alert message directive in your application: ```js @NgModule({ ... imports: [ ..., LgSrAlertMessageDirective ], }); ``` In your HTML, apply the directive to the relevant element. Pass in a boolean value to indicate when the message should be read out. When `true` the message will be read by the screen reader and will be removed after a set period (default is 8000ms). This directive can be used together with the `LgSpinnerComponent` to show a spinner while something is loading and then send a message to screen readers when it has finished loading. ```html # Display a spinner while loading @if (!loaded) { } # Tell the screen reader to read a message when loading is finished

Loading complete

``` ### Inputs | Name | Description | Type | Default | Required | |--------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|:---------:|:-------:|:--------:| | `lgSrAlertMessage` | Whether the message should be read or not | `boolean` | `null` | Yes | | `timer` | The time (in ms) that needs to pass before the message become hidden to screen readers | `number` | `8000` | No | *** {Feedback}

helpers-directives-show-at-guide--docs.content: import { Meta, Markdown, Source } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'; import Feedback from '../../docs/common/feedback.md?raw'; import * as ShowAtStories from './show-at.stories'; import { CustomBadge } from 'storybook-addon-tag-badges/manager-helpers' # Show at Due for brand modernisation This utility directive allows for mobile-first media queries to be added to components, so they can be shown at the desired breakpoint, without the need to write additional CSS. For example, you may need to show a component only when the viewport is wide enough, e.g. desktop. It uses global CSS responsive utility classes to add the relevant classes. It has a sibling directive: `LgHideAt`. ## Usage Import the show-at directive in your application: ```js @NgModule({ ... imports: [ ..., LgShowAtDirective ], }); ``` and in your HTML: ### Inputs The current available breakpoints are `sm`, `md`, `lg`, `xl`, and `xxl`. | Name | Description | Type | Default | Required | |------------|------------------------------------|:--------:|:-------:|:--------:| | `lgShowAt` | The name of the breakpoint applied | `string` | `null` | No | *** ## Related * [Hide at directive](./?path=/docs/helpers-directives-hide-at-guide--docs) {Feedback}

helpers-directives-skeleton-loading-guid.content: import { Meta, Markdown } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'; import Feedback from '../../docs/common/feedback.md?raw'; import { CustomBadge } from 'storybook-addon-tag-badges/manager-helpers' # Skeleton loading Due for brand modernisation The skeleton directive adds a skeleton loading state to a component or element. ## Usage Import the skeleton directive in your application: ```js @NgModule({ ... imports: [ ..., LgSkeletonDirective ], }); ``` The directive works on components that output text or projected content. On composite components that are made up of other child components, it is best to add the skeleton loading directive to the child components or even wrap the projected content inside another element. E.g. In the example, the `lg-data-point-label` projects the content into an `lg-heading` component, so the directive is added to a span wrapping the content. However, the `lg-data-point-value` component simply projects the content only so the directive can be applied to the component itself. ```html {{ data?.label }} {{ data?.value }} ``` ### Inputs | Name | Description | Type | Default | Required | |--------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|:--------:|:-------:|:--------:| | `lgSkeletonWidth` | Override the default width with the desired value in ems | `string` | `100%` | No | | `lgSkeletonHeight` | Override the default height with the desired value in ems | `string` | `auto` | No | *** {Feedback}

helpers-style-mixins--docs.content: import { Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks'; import { CustomBadge } from 'storybook-addon-tag-badges/manager-helpers' # Mixins Due for brand modernisation ## lg-inner-focus-outline Sets the focus outline inside the element, by using multiple box shadows. ### Arguments | Name | Description | Default | Required | | ----------- | ----------------------------------- | :-------------------------: | :------: | | `$bg-color` | The background colour of the element | n/a | Yes | | `$color` | The colour of the outline | `var(--colour-greyscale-0)` | No | ## lg-focus-outline Sets the focus outline outside the element, by using box shadows. This is currently used on buttons. ### Arguments | Name | Description | Default | Required | | ----------------- | ---------------------------------- | :--------------------------------: | :------: | | `$bg-color` | The outer colour of the box-shadow | `var(--default-focus-color)` | No | | `$inner-bg-color` | The inner colour of the box-shadow | `var(--default-inner-focus-color)` | No | ## lg-link Styles elements with the link style. ### Arguments | Name | Description | Default | Required | | ------------------ | ------------------------------------------- | :---------------------------: | :------: | | `$default-color` | The default colour of the element | `var(--link-primary-rest-colour)` | No | | `$hover-color` | The hover colour of the element | `var(--link-primary-hover-colour)` | No | | `$visited-color` | The visited colour of the element | `var(--link-primary-visited-colour)` | No | | `$active-color` | The active colour of the element | `var(--link-primary-active-colour)` | No | | `$focus-color` | The focus colour of the element | `var(--link-primary-focus-colour)` | No | | `$focus-border-width` | The focus border-width of the element | `var(--link-primary-focus-border-width)` | No | | `$focus-border-radius` | The focus border-radius of the element | `var(--link-primary-focus-border-radius)` | No | ## lg-unstyled-link Revert the links styles added with `lg-link`. ## lg-font-size Styles text to one of the predefined font sizes. '0-8' and '0-6' are the sub body font sizes. ### Arguments | Name | Description | Default | Required | | ----------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- | :---------------------------------------: | :------: | | `$size` | The font size (e.g. `7`, `6`, `5`, `4`, `3`, `2`, `1`, `0-8`, `0-6`) | n/a | Yes | | `$modifier` | Font weight modifier, (e.g. `300`, `400`, `500`, `700`) | `400` for Nunito Sans, `500` for ABC Otto | No | | `$type` | Font family type (e.g. 'expressive') | null | No | ## lg-visually-hidden Provides styles to hide information intended only for screen readers from the layout of the rendered page. ## lg-breakpoint Allows breakpoints to be added to css blocks. Supports min-width, max-width, and range-based media queries. ### Arguments | Name | Description | Default | Required | | ----------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | :----------: | :------: | | `$breakpoint` | The string value for the breakpoint screen size (e.g. `'sm'`, `'md'`, `'lg'`, `'xl'`, `'xxl'`) | n/a | Yes | | `$range` | The type of media query: `'min-width'`, `'max-width'`, or `'range'` | `'min-width'`| No | | `$max-breakpoint` | The maximum breakpoint name when using `$range: 'range'` (e.g. `'lg'` when `$breakpoint: 'md'`) | `null` | No | ### Examples ```scss // Min-width only (default) - applies from md breakpoint onwards .my-class { @include lg-breakpoint('md') { color: 'red'; } } // Max-width only - applies up to lg breakpoint .my-class { @include lg-breakpoint('lg', 'max-width') { color: 'blue'; } } // Range - applies only between md and lg breakpoints .my-class { @include lg-breakpoint('md', 'range', 'lg') { color: 'yellow'; } } ``` ### Notes - When using `'max-width'`, the mixin automatically subtracts `0.06rem` to prevent overlap with min-width queries - When using `'range'`, the mixin creates a combined `(min-width: X) and (max-width: Y)` media query - For the `sm` breakpoint with `'range'`, only `max-width` is used (since min-width would be 0) ## lg-status Styles components and native child elements to the specified status. ### Arguments | Name | Description | Default | Required | | ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ | :-------: | :------: | | `$status` | The status type (e.g. `generic`, `info`, `success`, `warning`, `error`) | `generic` | No | ## lg-content-area Applies content area styling with responsive border radius. The border radius increases at medium and large breakpoints. ### Example ```scss .my-content-area { @include lg-content-area; } ``` ### Notes - Uses `--container-border-radius-sm` by default - Applies `--container-border-radius-md` at `md` breakpoint - Applies `--container-border-radius-lg` at `lg` breakpoint - Includes background color from `--content-area-background-colour`
